attribute_description: Technology Readiness Level (TRL) on the standard 1–9 scale, where 1 = basic principles observed and 9 = commercial deployment. Integer value representing technological maturity, with progression across tech_year for non-mature technologies.

Aggregated maturity classification derived from Technology Readiness Level (TRL) scale (1-9), indicating developmental stage: Prototype (TRL 1-4), Emerging (TRL 5-7), Mature (TRL 8-9). Updated annually per tech_year parameter.

Overall energy conversion efficiency defined as the ratio of main output energy to main input energy, with operational bounds 0 < η ≤ 1. This dimensionless quantity is calculated using lower heating value (LHV) for chemical fuels and kilowatt-hours (kWh) for electricity and heat. It reflects the technical performance of the energy conversion process based on the primary energy input and the main output carrier's energy content.

Ratio of the main outputs energy content (LHV) to the main inputs energy content (LHV) at theoretical maximum, calculated as η = (output energy LHV) / (input energy LHV). Accounts for thermodynamic limits, idealized process conditions, and LHV energy convention. May be blank if not applicable.

Expected operational lifetime of an asset, typically expressed in years, used for depreciation and maintenance planning. Represents the period over which the asset is economically viable, considering factors like maintenance costs, technological obsolescence, and replacement schedules. Distinct from technical lifetime, which focuses on physical durability.

Fixed one-time capital expenditure independent of installed capacity, representing a per-project lump sum in Euros (EUR). This cost is not scaled with capacity and covers non-recurring project-specific investments.

Specific capital expenditure per unit of installed power capacity (EUR/kW), representing primary scaling parameter for investment costs in conversion technologies. Defines engineering bounds for CAPEX normalisation to nameplate capacity (kW) across all technologies.

Fixed one-time operational expenditure independent of capacity, representing non-recurring costs such as installation, commissioning, or initial maintenance. Expressed in Euros (EUR) and normalized per technology unit.

attribute_name: Fixed Operational Expenditure Percentage Of Capital Expenditure
attribute_description: Ratio of fixed annual operational expenditure (OPEX) to total capital expenditure (CAPEX), expressed as a percentage. Defines the proportion of CAPEX allocated to recurring operational costs (e.g., maintenance, labor, insurance) excluding energy and feedstock costs. Valid values represent 0–100% of CAPEX.

Fixed annual operational expenditure per unit of installed capacity, excluding fuel costs. Represents recurring annual costs for operation, maintenance, and consumables (excluding energy costs) normalised to installed capacity in kW. Expressed in Euros per kilowatt per year (EUR/kW/yr).

Variable operational expenditure per unit of energy or mass output, excluding fuel costs. Includes maintenance, labor, consumables, and other recurring operational costs associated with energy production or conversion processes.
The typical temperature range (in °C) at which the core process operates, including minimum and maximum bounds, with engineering specifications for thermal integration.

The smallest assumed unit capacity for the technology, expressed in kilowatts (kW) or kilograms per hour (kg/h), depending on the technology's primary output. A value of 0 indicates no minimum installation size constraint.

Qualitative uncertainty rating based on source reliability, expert judgment, and parameter variability. Reflects confidence intervals, data scarcity, and extrapolation risks across techno-economic parameters.

Technology-specific weighted average cost of capital (WACC) used to annualise capital expenditure (CAPEX) over the project lifetime. Defines the minimum acceptable return for investors, incorporating debt/equity financing costs and risk premiums. Typically ranges between 5-20% depending on technology maturity and regulatory environment.

The Reference Unit Size defines the nameplate capacity (in MW or t/h) used as a baseline for normalizing specific cost and performance data across technologies. It represents the standardized operational scale for techno-economic analysis, ensuring consistency in cost projections and efficiency calculations.

Specifies the energy or material carrier being stored, using abbreviations defined in the Carrier tab. This field categorizes the type of storage (e.g., H2 for hydrogen, CH4 for methane) and is linked to the Carrier lookup table for consistency and reference.

The smallest assumed storage capacity in kilowatt-hours (kWh), representing the minimum operational threshold for storage systems. This value defines the lower bound for storage capacity in engineering designs and accounting lines, ensuring feasibility and compliance with technical constraints. Must be a non-negative value.

Maximum charging power as a fraction of rated storage capacity (C-rate equivalent), representing the ratio of maximum charging power to the rated capacity. This dimensionless factor indicates the rate at which a storage device can be charged relative to its total capacity, typically ranging from 0 (no charging) to 1 (maximum charging rate).

Ratio of maximum discharge power to rated storage capacity, representing the fraction of capacity that can be discharged under optimal conditions. Defined by operational limits, system efficiency, and engineering constraints during energy release cycles.

Dimensionless ratio representing the proportion of input energy successfully stored during charging, bounded between 0 (no efficiency) and 1 (perfect efficiency). Calculated as (energy stored / energy input) accounting for losses in conversion, thermal dissipation, and system resistance.
The ratio of energy output during discharging to the energy input, representing the efficiency of the storage system during discharge. Values range from 0 to 1, where 1 indicates 100% efficiency.

The minimum state of charge (SOC) represents the lowest allowable energy level as a fraction of the total storage capacity, ensuring operational safety and system reliability. It is defined by engineering constraints, accounting for degradation limits and operational requirements.

The maximum state of charge (SOC) represents the upper limit of charge that a storage system can reach, expressed as a fraction (0 to 1) of its total energy capacity. This value defines the operational boundary for charging processes and is critical for system design and safety constraints.

Fractional energy loss per hour during standby mode, representing the rate of self-discharge or inefficiency when the system is not in active operation. This value is typically expressed as a dimensionless fraction (per hour) and is used to calculate energy losses over time.

Specific capital expenditure per unit of installed storage capacity, normalised to EUR per kWh of storage capacity. Represents primary scaling parameter for storage technologies (StorTech) under the specified cost base (ECA). Must adhere to non-negative values reflecting actual investment costs.

attribute_name: Annual Operational Expenditure Per Storage Capacity
attribute_description: Fixed annual operational expenditure per unit of storage capacity, expressed in Euros per kilowatt-hour per year. Must be a non-negative value.

Cradle-to-gate GHG emissions for direct air capture systems, measured in kgCO2eq per kg of CO2 captured, following PREMISE v2.3.6 + ecoinvent v3.11 LCA framework. Applies to RER (Europe) location context.
